CF-Rec: Collaborative Filtering with Neural Graph Diffusion for Recommendation

## 1 Introduction

Recommender systems rely on user-item interaction data to predict preferences. Traditional matrix factorization (MF) and neural collaborative filtering (NCF) treat users and items as independent vectors, ignoring the rich graph structure among items. We propose CF-Rec, a framework that combines collaborative filtering with neural graph diffusion to capture high-order item relationships. CF-Rec is designed to address data sparsity and cold-start in e-commerce recommendation.

## 2 Related Work

Collaborative filtering has evolved from matrix factorization (SVD, BPR-MF) to neural approaches (NCF, DeepMF) and graph-based methods (LightGCN, NGCF). Knowledge graph-enhanced recommenders (RippleNet, KGAT) incorporate side information but require extensive KG construction. Our work bridges these by treating the user-item bipartite graph as a dynamic diffusion process.

## 3 Method

CF-Rec has three components. The first is a graph diffusion layer that propagates user and item embeddings along the bipartite user-item graph, with attention-weighted aggregation of multi-hop neighbors. The second is a contrastive auxiliary loss that pulls together positive user-item pairs and pushes apart negative samples, mitigating sparsity. The third is a side-information encoder that fuses item category, brand, and price into the item embedding.

Formally, let G = (U ∪ V, E) be the bipartite user-item graph. The user embedding u_i is updated as u_i^(l+1) = aggregate({v_j : (i,j) ∈ E}) and similarly for v_j. After L layers, we concatenate embeddings across all layers to capture both local and global graph structure. The final score is the dot product of user and item embeddings.

## 4 Experiment

We evaluate CF-Rec on three public benchmarks: Amazon-Beauty (22k users, 12k items, 198k interactions), Yelp2018 (32k users, 18k items, 277k interactions), and MovieLens-1M (6k users, 4k items, 1M interactions). We use Recall@20 and NDCG@20 as the primary metrics, following the standard leave-one-out protocol with 100 negative samples per positive.

Baselines include BPR-MF, LightGCN, NGCF, and KGAT. All methods are trained with the same embedding size (64), learning rate (1e-3), and early stopping on validation Recall@20. We use Adam optimizer with L2 regularization coefficient 1e-4.

## 5 Result

CF-Rec achieves Recall@20 of 0.0654 on Amazon-Beauty (vs. 0.0583 for LightGCN), 0.0712 on Yelp2018 (vs. 0.0665 for LightGCN), and 0.1245 on MovieLens-1M (vs. 0.1187 for LightGCN). NDCG@20 follows a similar pattern, with improvements ranging from 4% to 9% over the strongest baseline. The contrastive auxiliary loss is the primary contributor, adding 2-4% Recall, while the side-information encoder adds 0.5-1% Recall on cold-start items.

## 6 Conclusion

CF-Rec combines collaborative filtering with neural graph diffusion, yielding consistent improvements over LightGCN and NGCF on three benchmarks. The contrastive loss mitigates sparsity, and the side-information encoder helps cold-start items. Future work will explore temporal dynamics and causal intervention for unbiased recommendation.
